ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Singaporean-style salad prawn; closest match is generic shrimp salad (FOODON_03311737). Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singaporean salad prawn; A0CKP (crustaceans, prepared) is the closest defensible anchor. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for salad prawn dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: dressed with salad dressing or mayonnaise

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of salad prawn in Singapore is approximately 200 g, comprising chilled cooked prawns arranged on a bed of salad vegetables with dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Salad Prawn — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Salad Prawn (cr_id G00010772), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on mixed seafood salad with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean salad prawn. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood and salad vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are salad vegetables and any dressing; prawns themselves have negligible GI impact. GL per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (7.92 g/100g × 200 g serving / 100) = 45 × 15.84 / 100 ≈ 7.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 502 mg/100g with low potassium offset (184 mg/100g) and minimal dietary fibre (0.4 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium dressings such as mayonnaise or soy sauce, as the 502 mg sodium per 100 g already approaches DASH daily meal thresholds; request herb and lemon dressing as a lower-sodium alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low-moderate carbohydrate (7.92 g/100g), low sugar (2.96 g/100g), high protein (13.24 g/100g), and estimated low GI (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salad prawn is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low-moderate carbohydrate content, high protein which slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glycaemic response, and estimated low GI; avoid sweet cocktail sauces or high-sugar dressings that would elevate glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 119 mg/100g and sodium at 502 mg/100g; however, total fat is low (1.22 g/100g) and saturated fat is minimal (~0.3 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the 119 mg/100g dietary cholesterol from prawns and the elevated sodium content; the very low saturated fat and absence of trans fat are favourable, and omega-3 fatty acids in prawns provide anti-inflammatory cardiovascular benefit — choose olive oil or lemon dressing and limit portion to 100–150 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) from prawns reduce joint inflammation; very low saturated fat; nightshade vegetables (tomato) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salad prawn is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to anti-inflammatory omega-3 content in prawns; individuals sensitive to nightshades should monitor symptom response to tomato-containing preparations and request olive oil and lemon dressing to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salad Prawn (沙拉虾) is a popular Singaporean Chinese-style cold seafood dish consisting of chilled cooked prawns — typically boiled or steamed — served on a bed of fresh salad vegetables such as lettuce, tomato, and cucumber, dressed with mayonnaise, Thousand Island dressing, or a lighter lemon-herb vinaigrette. The dish is light in texture with a clean, sweet prawn flavour contrasted by the crisp freshness of the salad vegetables and the creamy or tangy dressing. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish at Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and family gatherings in Singapore, and is particularly popular at festive occasions and banquets. Nutritionally, salad prawn is a high-protein, low-fat dish: per 100 g it provides 99 kcal, 13.24 g protein, 1.22 g fat, and 7.92 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 502 mg/100g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. Cholesterol at 119 mg/100g is notable for cardiovascular monitoring. The dish has an estimated low glycaemic index (~45) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when prepared without sweet dressings. Prawns are a source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), supporting anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular health benefits. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-friendly when prepared without non-halal additives.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salad prawn?
Is prawn salad good for diabetics?
Can I eat salad prawn if I have high blood pressure?
What is the sodium content of salad prawn?
Is salad prawn high in cholesterol?
What dressing is used in salad prawn?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Salad Prawn (cr_id G00010772) with confidence 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and provenance is canonical. Saturated fat estimated at ~0.3 g/100g based on typical prawn lipid profile from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, shrimp cooked) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.3 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked shrimp (NDB 15152), as HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. Trans fat set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible for boiled/steamed prawn preparations). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo for this dish entry; USDA SR Legacy reports MUFA ~0.18 g and PUFA ~0.44 g per 100g for plain cooked shrimp but these values are not directly applicable to the composite salad dish and are therefore withheld. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for Salad Prawn and no approved composite-dish source provides reliable values for this specific preparation. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed seafood and salad vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean salad prawn has been published. 5. The input record name 'Sald Prawn' is a typographic variant; canonical English name corrected to 'Salad Prawn'. 6. Cholesterol at 119 mg/100g is notable — individuals with prescribed dietary cholesterol limits should monitor total intake. 7. Halal status is conditional on dressing used; mayonnaise must be from halal-certified source.
